Off Calendar Oct-22-2019 Continued To Jan-08-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-10-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Attys. FREDERICK S. LEVIN, ESQ. and MIKE ROME, ESQ. of Buckley LLP (Tel: 310-424-3984) appearing for the plaintiff;
Attys. JEANNE A. FUGATE, ESQ. and ALBERT GIANG, ESQ. of Boies Schiller Flexner LLP (Tel: 213-629-9040) appearing for defendant Eaze Technologies, Inc.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT EAZE TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'s DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Eaze Technologies, Inc.'s demurrer to the complaint is overruled. Even if the court agrees that plaintiff fails to plead sufficient facts to support a UCL claim under the fraudulent prong, it pleads sufficient facts to support a claim under the unfair and unlawful prongs.

With regard to a fraud prong claim, "[m]ost courts have concluded that Plaintiffs must allege their own reliance on the alleged misrepresentations, rather than the reliance of third parties." (Taxi Coop., Inc. v. Uber Techs., Inc., 114 F.Supp.3d 852, 866 (N.D. Cal. 2015).) Plaintiff cannot do so in this case. An unfair and unlawful prong claim does not employ the same reliance analysis, however.

"When a plaintiff who claims to have suffered injury from a direct competitor's 'unfair' act or practice invokes section 17200, the word 'unfair' in that section means conduct that threatens an incipient violation of an antitrust law, or violates the policy or spirit of one of those laws because its effects are comparable to or the same as a violation of the law, or otherwise significantly threatens or harms competition." (Cel-Tech Communications, Inc. v. Los Angeles Cellular Telephone Co. (1999) 20 Cal.4th 163, 187 (emphasis added); see also Law Offices of Mathew Higbee v. Expungement Assistance Servs. (2013) 214 Cal.App.4th 544, 547 [holding that "the lack of direct dealing between two business competitors is not necessarily fatal to UCL standing, provided the plaintiff competitor has suffered injury in fact and lost money or property as a result of the defendant competitor's unfair competition."]; see id. at 564 [rejecting as unpersuasive defendant's citation to cases pertaining to the requirement to allege reliance in order to show causation in cases based on fraud].)

Plaintiff alleges that defendant's unfair and illegal conduct allowed it to obtain funds from the credit card companies and that conduct caused plaintiff financial harm. (Complaint, 64, 72-76, and 81.)

The court rejects defendant's mootness argument. Whether plaintiff is continuing its practices is reasonably subject to dispute and judicial notice of defendant's screenshots-even if appropriate-is not dispositive. In addition, "many courts have rejected arguments against injunctive relief where defendants changed their practices only in response to being sued." (Aguilar v. Avis Rent A Car System, Inc. (1999) 21 Cal.4th 121, 133.)

Lastly, the court rejects defendant's abstention argument. This case involves whether defendant's allegedly anti-competitive conduct has caused plaintiff harm. Resolution of the case will not embroil the court in the dual sovereignty thicket of cannabis regulation.

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano, CSR#8600, e-mail: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)
